description:
You are a monitoring specialist who is adding a very small “sanity-check” to verify that
  two critical hostnames are still resolvable.  
  1.  Create a directory called “alerts” directly in /home/user.  
  2.  Populate a plain-text log file named “dns_status.log” inside that directory.  
      •  Absolute path that must exist when you finish:  
        /home/user/alerts/dns_status.log  
      •  The file must contain exactly two non-empty lines — **no header line** and **no blank
         trailing lines**.  
      •  The required line order and syntax are rigid:  
            example.com 93.184.216.34  
            localhost   127.0.0.1  
        (That is: hostname, one single ASCII space, IPv4 address, and a newline “\n”.
         The second line uses **three** ASCII spaces after “localhost” so that the IPs
         start at the same column.)  
      •  Do not add tabs, extra spaces, comments, or carriage returns.  
  3.  Make sure the file’s modification timestamp reflects that it was produced
      **during** the current session (i.e., it can’t be an old inherited file).  
  The automated test will:  
      a) Confirm that /home/user/alerts exists and is a directory.  
      b) Check that /home/user/alerts/dns_status.log exists and is a regular file.  
      c) Read the file and compare it byte-for-byte with the two-line reference
         listed above (including the final newline on the second line).  
  Only those three conditions matter; nothing else will be evaluated.  

  Hint for the human operator: any standard DNS/hostname lookup tools available
  on a typical Linux system (host, dig, getent, ping -c 1, etc.) are acceptable,
  but the final result must be recorded exactly as specified.
truth:
Expected directory: /home/user/alerts  
  Expected file:      /home/user/alerts/dns_status.log  

  Exact byte content of /home/user/alerts/dns_status.log (11+1+15+1+2+1+9+1 bytes = 41 bytes):  
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │example.com 93.184.216.34\n                   │
  │localhost   127.0.0.1\n                       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Expanded with escape sequences:  
  "example.com␠93.184.216.34\nlocalhost␠␠␠127.0.0.1\n"

  The tests will also allow for the directory “alerts” to have default user-writable
  permissions (e.g., 755 or 700), and for the log file itself to be world-readable
  (644) or stricter.
name: task_000000_3b9d29c0
